title: Westminster Larger Catechism Questions 134-136: The Sixth Commandment
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch

outline.title: Introduction: Every Commandment Contains a Family of Sins and Duties
subpoints: Joel Beeke's observation: each commandment contains a family of sins
The sixth commandment applies not just to literal murder but to hatred in the heart (1 John 3:15)
Our sin leads us to cling to Christ who kept the law perfectly

outline.title: WLC Q135: Duties Required in the Sixth Commandment
subpoints: Careful studies and lawful endeavors to preserve life — of ourselves and others
Resisting all thoughts, subduing all passions, avoiding occasions that tend to unjust taking of life
Just defense against violence (self-defense of self and others)
Patient bearing of the hand of God, quietness of mind, cheerfulness of spirit
Sober use of meat, drink, medicine, sleep, labor, and recreations
Charitable thoughts, love, compassion, meekness, gentleness, kindness
Peaceable speeches and behavior, forbearance, readiness to be reconciled
Comforting and succoring the distressed, protecting and defending the innocent

outline.title: Self-Defense and the Sixth Commandment
subpoints: The magistrate's duty to protect life by the sword (Romans 13:4; Belgic Confession Art. 36)
The private individual's right to defend life (Prov 24:11-12; Luke 22:36-38)
Jesus rebuked Peter not for bearing a sword but for murderous intent (Matt 26:52)
Matthew 5:39 (turn the other cheek) addresses verbal slander, not lethal self-defense

outline.title: Patient Bearing and the Sober Use of Created Goods
subpoints: Patient submission to God's sovereign hand is a duty under the sixth commandment
Job 38-42: God's questioning of Job illustrates our creaturely dependence
Sober use of food, drink, medicine, sleep, labor, and recreation preserves life
Gluttony, drunkenness, overwork, and poor sleep violate this duty

outline.title: WLC Q136: Sins Forbidden in the Sixth Commandment
subpoints: All unjust taking of life — except capital punishment, lawful war, and necessary defense
Neglecting or withdrawing lawful and necessary means of preserving life
Sinful anger, hatred, envy, desire of revenge, excessive passions
Provoking words, oppression, quarreling, striking, wounding
Capital punishment commanded for murder (Gen 9:6); magistrate who fails to wield the sword sins
The parable of the Good Samaritan: the priest and Levite sinned by omission

summary:
Mr. Isaiah Couch teaches through Westminster Larger Catechism questions 134-136, covering the sixth commandment, "Thou shalt not kill." He opens by observing that each of the Ten Commandments contains both a family of sins and a family of duties, and that closer examination always reveals how far short we fall — which drives us to Christ, who alone kept the commandment perfectly. The lesson demonstrates that the sixth commandment reaches far beyond the prohibition of literal murder (1 John 3:15: "everyone who hates his brother is a murderer") to encompass an active, comprehensive duty to preserve life.

The positive duties of the sixth commandment receive extensive treatment: careful research and planning to preserve life (illustrated by Joseph's preparation for the Egyptian famine), just defense against violence by both the magistrate and the private individual, patient endurance of God's providential hand, and sober use of food, drink, medicine, sleep, labor, and recreation. Mr. Couch engages the question of self-defense in depth, citing Luke 22:36-38 (Jesus directing his disciples to buy swords), Proverbs 24:11-12, and Matthew 26:52, and arguing that Jesus rebuked Peter not for bearing a sword but for the murderous intent of his heart. He distinguishes between Matthew 5:39 ("turn the other cheek"), which addresses verbal insult rather than lethal threat, and genuine self-defense.

The lesson concludes by walking through the sins forbidden in the sixth commandment: unjust taking of life (except in the cases of capital punishment, lawful war, and necessary defense), neglect of the means of preserving life, sinful anger and hatred, provoking words, and all acts tending toward the destruction of life. Capital punishment is affirmed as a biblical requirement for murder (Genesis 9:6), grounded in the offense against the image of God. The teacher closes with an appeal to repentance and dependence on Christ.
transcript:
## Introduction

Happy Lord's Day to you all. It is once again a great joy to be able to speak during the Sunday School hour. So I thank the session for allowing me to do so. This morning we are continuing our series through the moral law, and we come to the sixth commandment, using the Westminster Larger Catechism as our guide. We will be addressing the duties required and the sins forbidden in this commandment.

As an introduction, the question is asked in the Larger Catechism: which is the sixth commandment? The sixth commandment is, "Thou shalt not kill."

Joel Beeke once said that when we closely examine each of these ten words that God has given us, we find within it a family of sins. And I would even say that we find a family of duties as well. In the first commandment, we do not love God as we ought. In the second, we do not worship him as he has commanded. In the third, we take his name in vain. In the fourth, we claim his holy day for ourselves. In the fifth, we dishonor our father and mother. And in the sixth this morning, we murder ourselves and others.

When we come to "thou shalt not kill," we might be tempted to defend ourselves and think that we have not murdered anyone, indeed. But the word of Scripture rings in our ears: "Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer" (1 John 3:15). By these words we know that we have all fallen short. But as I have said before in this series, this knowledge of our sin and misery leads us to rejoice and cling to Christ. To fix our eyes upon the great deliverance we have through his life, death, resurrection, ascension, and rule — and one day his return.

## WLC Q135: Duties Required in the Sixth Commandment

Question 135 asks, "What are the duties required in the sixth commandment?" The answer: the duties required in the sixth commandment are all careful studies and lawful endeavors to preserve the life of ourselves and others, by resisting all thoughts and purposes, subduing all passions and avoiding all occasions, temptations, and practices which tend to the unjust taking away the life of any; by just defense thereof against violence; patient bearing of the hand of God; quietness of mind; cheerfulness of spirit; a sober use of meat, drink, physics, sleep, labor, and recreations; by charitable thoughts, love, compassion, meekness, gentleness, and kindness; peaceable, mild, and courteous speeches and behavior; forbearance, readiness to be reconciled, patient bearing and forgiving of injuries; and requiring good for evil; comforting and succoring the distressed; and protecting and defending the innocent.

When I first began to really study the Ten Commandments using the Larger Catechism, I never really thought the sixth commandment was anything more than a prohibition against murder. This is where the Larger Catechism is incredibly helpful, showing that the duties required in Scripture extend far beyond the prohibition of taking life unjustly, but include the active seeking to preserve our life and the life of others in our thoughts, our words, and our deeds.

### Preserving Life: Research, Planning, and Provision

The Catechism begins with all careful studies and lawful endeavors to preserve the life of ourselves and others. Ephesians 5:29 says that no one ever hated his own flesh, but nourishes it and cherishes it just as the Lord does the church. God created man to live. We see this in Adam giving his wife the name Eve — mother of all living (Gen 3:20) — and in the command to be fruitful and multiply (Gen 1:28).

Joseph's response to the dream of Pharaoh is a prime example: he researched and planned for the famine that would last seven years, storing grain throughout the land of Egypt (Gen 41). His careful studies and lawful endeavors preserved not only his life but the life of others — including his brothers, to whom he later said, "I will provide for you and your little ones" (Gen 50:21).

James 2:15 warns against saying to a destitute brother, "Depart in peace, be warmed and filled," without giving them what they need. And in Matthew 25, Jesus declares that when we fed the hungry, clothed the naked, visited the sick, and welcomed the stranger, we did it unto him.

Practically, this duty includes the fields of medicine, infrastructure (roads and traffic safety), and emergency response — all of which serve the lawful preservation of life.

### Avoiding Occasions and Temptations

Deuteronomy 22:8 commands that when you build a new house, you shall make a parapet for your roof, lest anyone fall from it. The principle: we should actively remove occasions, temptations, and practices which tend to the unjust taking of life. Proverbs 1:10-16 warns the son not to consent when sinners entice him to ambush the innocent; Psalm 1:1-2 describes the blessed man as one who does not walk in the counsel of the ungodly. These passages teach us to avoid the company and the paths that lead to destruction.

### Just Defense Against Violence

The Belgic Confession, Article 36, teaches that God appointed kings, princes, and magistrates to restrain the dissoluteness of men, investing them with the sword for the punishment of evildoers and the protection of those who do well — drawn from Romans 13:4: "He does not bear the sword in vain, for he is God's minister and avenger to execute wrath on him who practices evil."

The question arises: what about the private individual whose life or the life of his family is threatened? Proverbs 24:11-12 commands us to "deliver those who are drawn toward death and hold back those stumbling to the slaughter." In Luke 22:36-38, Jesus directed his disciples to buy swords — an instruction that appears to be for no other purpose than self-defense. When Jesus rebuked Peter in the garden (Matt 26:52), he did not say to throw away his sword — he rebuked Peter's murderous intent toward a man who was not himself seeking to take someone's life unjustly. Matthew 5:39 ("turn the other cheek") addresses verbal insult or slander, not lethal self-defense — to read it otherwise creates a contradiction within the sixth commandment itself.

### Patient Bearing of the Hand of God

The duty here is patience and submission to the will of God in his sovereign hand. J. G. Voss remarks that when the Christian gives way to impatience and rebellion against God's will, he is conducting himself to some extent like the reprobate in hell. It is the duty of the Christian to face life serenely and cheerfully in spite of disappointments, sufferings, and hardships.

James 5:7-11 calls us to be patient until the coming of the Lord, reminding us of the perseverance of Job and the compassion of the Lord. Job 38-42, in which God questions Job for four chapters and Job responds, "I have heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you. Therefore I abhor myself and repent in dust and ashes," is the greatest scriptural passage for destroying the sin of impatience rising in the heart.

The Heidelberg Catechism reminds us: "I am not my own, but belong in both body and soul, in life and in death, to my faithful Savior Jesus Christ."

### Sober Use of Meat, Drink, Medicine, Sleep, Labor, and Recreation

Proverbs 23:19-21 warns against wine bibbers and gluttonous eaters of meat: "The drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty." The duties here are the conscientious and life-preserving use of things that will not have a harmful effect on our physical or mental health. The Catechism is not saying these things are sinful in themselves, but that they must be used soberly. The speaker candidly shared his own former struggles with lack of self-control in food and overwork, which took a toll on his physical and mental health, before he took seriously the duty of the sixth commandment to care for himself so that he might provide for his family as prophet, priest, and king.

### Charitable Thoughts, Love, and Kindness

The closing list of duties — charitable thoughts, love, compassion, meekness, gentleness, kindness, peaceable and courteous speeches, forbearance, readiness to be reconciled, forgiving of injuries, requiring good for evil, comforting the distressed, and defending the innocent — mirrors the fruit of the Spirit (Gal 5:22-25). The heart of these duties is love for God and love for others (1 John 5:2-3).

## WLC Q136: Sins Forbidden in the Sixth Commandment

The sins forbidden in the sixth commandment are: all taking away the life of ourselves or of others, except in case of public justice, lawful war, and necessary defense; the neglecting or withdrawing the lawful and necessary means of preserving life; sinful anger, hatred, envy, desire of revenge; all excessive passions; distracting cares; immoderate use of meat, drink, labor, and recreations; provoking words, oppression, quarreling, striking, wounding; and whatsoever tends to the destruction of the life of any.

### Capital Punishment

Genesis 9:6 commands that "whoever sheds man's blood, by man his blood shall be shed, for in the image of God he made man." J. G. Voss states: murder affronts God by destroying an image-bearer. The murderer commits sacrilege by failing to regard the image of God in man as something sacred. When the magistrate refuses or neglects to wield the sword of justice, he violates the sixth commandment by throwing away the instrument God gave him to preserve life.

The parable of the Good Samaritan illustrates the sin of omission: both the priest and the Levite, seeing a man beaten half to death, refused to conform to the law of God by not loving their neighbor as themselves. When we refuse to lawfully preserve our own life or the life of others, it is sin.

## Closing Prayer

Almighty God, we thank you for the sixth commandment that you have given us. We pray that by the power of your Spirit, he would cause us to fulfill the duties required. We know that we cannot keep those perfectly, and we look to Christ alone who has kept them perfectly. But we pray that we will be obedient to your word in seeking to glorify you in our life by loving others as you have loved us. I ask that you would bless the rest of your Lord's Day. I pray all this in your name. Amen.
